strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.376) because its three items tap somewhat distinct facets: local belonging (p3_2), national pride (p6), and tradition preservation (p8). These do not co-vary strongly enough on their own. From the unused items, the most conceptually aligned additions are: p5_1a (primary emotion felt about Mexico — directly taps affective orientation toward the nation, the core of national identity), p7 (self-identification/personal characterization — likely captures ethnic or national self-identification), and p78 (opinion on whether government should maintain Mexican Revolution ideas — taps symbolic attachment to Mexican national heritage/identity narrative). p85 (willingness to ask favors from Virgin of Guadalupe or saints) also reflects a distinctly Mexican cultural-identity practice but risks introducing a religiosity dimension. p8 (tradition preservation) is the weakest link to pure affective national pride and could be dropped if it continues to depress α, but it is retained tentatively as it anchors cultural identity. p3_2 (local belonging) is the most likely misfit since local attachment does not necessarily co-vary with national pride; however, it is retained as a sub-national identity anchor. The strongest additions are p5_1a and p7, which directly measure affective and self-identificatory dimensions of national identity. p78 adds a symbolic/heritage dimension. Together, p6 + p5_1a + p7 + p8 + p78 form a more coherent affective-symbolic national identity cluster. p3_2 is flagged for removal as local belonging is conceptually and empirically distinct from national pride.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the strength of emotional attachment, pride, and symbolic identification with being Mexican, including primary emotions felt toward Mexico, self-identification as Mexican, pride in national identity, importance of preserving cultural traditions, and attachment to the ideological heritage of the Mexican nation. Captures the affective and symbolic core of national identity.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct has extremely poor internal consistency (α = 0.141), likely because it mixes a general belief item (p50_1: most people are honest) with two very different referent-specific trust scales (neighbors vs. police). Trust in police is an institutional trust measure that often correlates poorly with interpersonal/neighbor trust in Latin American contexts, explaining the near-zero alpha. From the unused items, the best candidates for a coherent 'social trust and civic cooperation' cluster are: p48 (willingness to identify a perpetrator — civic trust behavior), p49 (willingness to testify in court — civic trust behavior), p40 (willingness to report information to authorities — trust in justice system), and p46_1 (perceived prevalence of Mexicans buying stolen goods — generalized social trust/honesty perception). These items all tap into the same underlying dimension: confidence in others and willingness to engage with formal institutions for collective benefit. p50_1 (general honesty belief) fits well with p46_1 as both measure generalized social trust perceptions. p48 and p49 are strongly conceptually linked (same scenario, sequential questions) and should correlate highly. p40 also reflects trust that authorities will act on information. p52_1 (trust in police) is the most problematic misfit — institutional trust in police is a distinct dimension from generalized social trust and civic cooperation — and should be removed. p51_1 (neighbor trust) can be retained as it anchors interpersonal trust. The proposed cluster (p50_1, p51_1, p40, p48, p49) focuses on generalized social trust and civic cooperation behaviors, which should yield better coherence. p46_1 could substitute for p52_1 if needed. Confidence is medium because without actual correlation data we cannot guarantee α ≥ 0.5, but the conceptual alignment is substantially stronger than the original set.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which respondents trust others in general and are willing to cooperate with formal institutions for collective justice — including belief in others' honesty, trust in neighbors, willingness to report crimes, and willingness to participate in judicial processes as a witness or identifier of perpetrators.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.405) partly because p19 (binary yes/no on economic security in old age) and p30_1 (control over economic situation specifically) are narrower and more concrete than the latent dimension of personal agency/optimism. The strongest candidates from unused items are: p74 ('qué tanto cree usted que puede decidir su futuro' — 0-10 scale on perceived ability to decide one's future) which directly taps personal agency/locus of control and should correlate strongly with p20 and p30_1; p28 or p29 (overall happiness, categorical and 0-10 scale respectively) which complement p24 (life satisfaction) and form a subjective well-being facet; and p75_1 (agreement that difficult problems depend only on God) which is a reverse-scored external locus of control item that directly contrasts with personal agency. Together, p20, p24, p74, p29, and p75_1 (reverse-coded) form a theoretically coherent personal agency + subjective well-being cluster. p19 should be removed because it is binary and taps a specific domain (retirement savings) rather than general optimism/agency, introducing method variance and conceptual mismatch. p30_1 can be retained as a domain-specific control perception that anchors the economic dimension, but if parsimony is needed it is the weakest link. p28 and p29 are near-duplicates; p29 (0-10 numeric) is preferred for its metric properties and better correlation potential with other scales. Adding p74 is the highest-priority change as it is the most direct measure of perceived personal agency over one's future, conceptually central to the construct. p75_1 adds a reverse-coded external attribution item that should increase discriminant spread and internal consistency.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' perceived ability to determine their own future, confidence in achieving cherished goals, subjective happiness and life satisfaction, and the degree to which they attribute life outcomes to personal versus external (divine) forces, capturing a latent dimension of internal locus of control, personal efficacy, and subjective well-being.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with the current cluster is that p6 and p7 are nominal/categorical 'pick the most important problem' items, while p4 is an ordinal severity rating. These different response formats and measurement approaches undermine internal consistency. The most coherent fix is to: (1) REMOVE p6 and p7, which are forced-choice nominal items measuring problem salience through selection rather than attitude intensity — they correlate poorly with Likert-style severity ratings; (2) ADD p5, which directly parallels p4 (local vs. national environmental situation perception) using the same response scale, creating a coherent macro-micro severity perception pair; (3) ADD p30_1, which measures concern intensity about a specific environmental issue (fossil fuels/energy), aligning with the affective salience dimension; (4) ADD p32, which captures perceived public awareness of environmental issues — a cognitive salience indicator that complements personal concern. Together, p4 + p5 + p30_1 + p32 form a more homogeneous cluster around 'perceived environmental problem salience' using consistent ordinal/Likert-type scales. p6 and p7 are misfit because nominal 'most important problem' selections do not covary reliably with attitude-intensity items. p12_1 was considered but measures policy preference rather than problem salience. p1_1 and p2 are general issue prioritization items not specific to environmental severity perception.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Captures perceived severity of environmental problems at national and local levels, concern about specific environmental threats (fossil fuel impacts), and awareness of how informed the public is about environmental issues — all measured on consistent ordinal intensity scales.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with the current cluster is that p13 and p14 ask about preferred policy mechanisms (likely nominal/categorical responses), while p41 asks a yes/no opinion about authority consideration — these response formats and conceptual levels are too heterogeneous to cohere. From the unused items, p12_1 (government should control natural resource exploitation) is the strongest addition: it is a Likert-scale attitude item directly measuring state intervention in environmental governance, closely aligned with the construct's theoretical core. p40 (who should be responsible for environmental protection) is also highly relevant — it directly operationalizes the 'responsibility attribution' dimension described in the construct. p32 (perception of public environmental awareness) is tangentially related to governance attitudes but weaker. The recommended action is to ADD p12_1 and p40, and REMOVE p13 and p14, which are likely categorical 'best measure' questions that introduce format-driven noise and measure policy preferences rather than governance attitudes/beliefs. Retaining p41 alongside p12_1 and p40 gives three attitudinal items with more compatible response formats (agreement/opinion scales) all centered on who bears responsibility and what role the state should play — the construct's stated core. This should substantially improve internal consistency. p14 is removed because asking individuals what families should do taps personal behavioral norms rather than governance orientation. p13 is removed because selecting a 'best measure for companies' is a policy preference task, not an attitude toward governance responsibility.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Captures attitudes about who bears responsibility for environmental protection — governments, corporations, or individuals — and the degree to which respondents endorse state regulatory authority over natural resources and environmental decision-making.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current cluster suffers from poor internal consistency (α = 0.362) because its items tap very different facets: personal impact (p37), social equity belief (p38_1), behavioral intention under risk (p39), and pollution harmfulness (p10_1). These do not cohere around a single latent dimension. From the unused pool, three items align well with a core 'climate change risk perception and concern' dimension: p27_1 (belief about the cause of climate change — a factual risk-belief item), p30_1 (concern about environmental effects of fossil fuels — affective risk appraisal), and p5 (perception of local environmental situation — proximal environmental threat assessment). Together with p37 (personal impact of climate change) and p38_1 (differential exposure of the poor), these five items share a common thread of perceived environmental/climate threat. Items recommended for removal: p39 (willingness to relocate is a behavioral intention, not a risk perception) and p10_1 (car pollution harmfulness is a general environmental attitude, not specific to climate change risk perception). p27_1 is a misconception item but still loads on the 'climate change belief' dimension and can anchor the scale's factual end. p30_1 and p5 add affective concern and local salience, which are theoretically central to risk perception. This revised set of 5 items (p37, p38_1, p27_1, p30_1, p5) is more conceptually homogeneous and should yield improved internal consistency, though confidence is medium given we cannot run the actual alpha without data.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which respondents perceive climate change and environmental degradation as real and threatening, including beliefs about climate change causes, concern about fossil fuel impacts, perception of the local environmental situation, personal experience of climate change effects, and awareness of differential vulnerability among poor populations.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current cluster has extremely poor internal consistency (α = 0.252) because its three items tap fundamentally different cognitive operations: p25 asks for a personal definition of poverty (open-ended conceptual), p39 asks who is the 'enemy' of the poor (a blame/adversarial framing), and p40 asks about responsibility for social problems broadly — these do not co-vary reliably. The fix is to build a tighter cluster around causal attribution and structural vs. individual explanations for poverty specifically. From the unused items, the strongest candidates are: p27_1 (first-mentioned reason why poverty exists — directly measures causal attribution), p31 (what is needed to end poverty — taps solution-oriented attribution, structural vs. individual), p35 (agreement that 'who is born poor almost always dies poor' — a structural determinism belief that correlates strongly with attribution style), and p30 (preferred partner for poverty reduction — reflects locus of responsibility). Together these four items all ask the respondent to reason about the causes, persistence, and solutions to poverty, which should produce much higher inter-item covariance. p26 (definition of extreme poverty) is also worth retaining as a conceptual anchor alongside p25, but open-ended definitional items are the weakest contributors to α; if the goal is psychometric coherence, they should be dropped. p39 ('main enemy of the poor') is idiosyncratic and adversarial in framing, unlikely to correlate with the attribution items, so it should be removed. p40 (responsibility for social problems broadly) is slightly more defensible but still too broad; it can be retained tentatively but is the weakest keeper. The recommended final cluster is: p27_1, p31, p35, p30, and optionally p40 — all focused on causal attribution, structural determinism, and locus of responsibility for poverty specifically.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Respondents' causal attributions for the existence and persistence of poverty, including first-mentioned reasons why poverty exists, beliefs about structural determinism (born poor/die poor), opinions on what is required to end poverty, and preferred locus of responsibility for poverty reduction (individual, government, civil society, etc.). Items share a common cognitive task of assigning cause and responsibility specifically to the poverty phenomenon, expected to yield substantially higher internal consistency than the prior definitional cluster.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current four items (p41–p44) span very different response formats and cognitive tasks—choosing a guiding principle, selecting a funding mechanism, rating program effectiveness, and agreeing with a vague phrase—which explains the near-zero internal consistency. They do not share a common latent variable. From the unused pool, several items converge on a coherent normative/attitudinal dimension about the *causes of poverty, government responsibility, and collective action to address poverty*: p27_1 (why poverty exists), p29_1 (government spending priority when resources are scarce), p30 (preferred partner for poverty reduction), p31 (what is needed to end poverty), p34 (how citizens can best contribute to social problems), and p35 (belief that those born poor die poor). These items all tap the same underlying construct: beliefs about structural vs. individual responsibility for poverty and preferred societal responses. p43 (perceived effectiveness of social programs) is the only current item that fits this reframed dimension and should be retained. p41, p42, and p44 are misfit items—they measure distinct, unrelated preferences (funding mechanisms, abstract principles, vague agreement) that do not co-vary with the attitudinal core—and should be removed. The proposed cluster of 5–6 items (p43, p27_1, p29_1, p31, p35, optionally p30) shares a common latent structure around poverty attribution and collective responsibility, which should yield substantially higher internal consistency. p34 is included as a borderline addition given its focus on citizen agency in social problem reduction, which aligns with the responsibility dimension.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Beliefs about the causes of poverty, the locus of responsibility for addressing it (government, citizens, collective actors), and normative views on what is needed to end poverty—capturing the structural vs. individual attribution dimension and preferred societal responses to poverty.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current three items (p32, p33, p37) suffer from near-zero or negative alpha because p33 asks about the respondent's *personal* treatment (first/second class), while p32 and p37 ask about *general societal beliefs* — mixing self-referential and society-referential frames collapses internal consistency. The fix is to (1) remove p33, which is the clear misfit (idiosyncratic personal experience vs. shared belief), and (2) add items that share the same attitudinal/belief frame about structural inequality and social immobility. From the unused pool, p35 ('quien nace pobre casi siempre muere pobre') directly taps belief in rigid social stratification — the same latent dimension as p32 and p37. p21_2 (whether most Mexicans can access quality public health services) taps perceived systemic inequality in access to public goods, which is conceptually adjacent and likely to co-vary with beliefs about discrimination and class hierarchy. Together p32 + p37 + p35 + p21_2 form a coherent attitudinal cluster around 'perceived structural inequality and social closure in Mexico,' all framed as general societal beliefs rather than personal experience. p33 is removed as the sole self-referential item that breaks the shared variance structure. Confidence is medium because alpha improvement is estimated from conceptual alignment without empirical pilot data; p21_2 is the weakest addition and could be dropped if it does not load cleanly.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Attitudinal beliefs about the existence and rigidity of social stratification in Mexico, including whether first- and second-class citizens exist, whether the poor face discrimination, whether social mobility is possible for those born poor, and whether most Mexicans have equitable access to public services. All items are framed as general societal beliefs rather than personal experience.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency (α=0.486) because it conflates two distinct sub-dimensions: (1) trust in electoral institutions (p41, p42, p43) and (2) personal/experiential vote-buying items (p71, p72). The vote-buying items (p71, p72) tap into observed/experienced corruption behavior rather than institutional trust attitudes, creating conceptual noise. To build a coherent institutional trust cluster, I recommend: REMOVING p71 and p72 (vote-buying items — these belong better in a political corruption/clientelism construct); ADDING p81_1 (trust in neighbors, 0-10 scale) and p82_1 (trust in police, 0-10 scale), which are classic social/institutional trust measures that align with the construct's stated goal of capturing 'faith in formal rules of the political game' and interpersonal civic trust; ADDING p40 (belief in whether the constitution is upheld in Mexico), which directly measures confidence in formal institutional rules; and ADDING p34 (rating of how democratically Mexico is currently governed, 0-10 scale), which captures overall institutional performance confidence. Together, p41 (election quality), p42 (INE guarantees clean elections), p43 (INE improving state elections), p81_1 (trust in neighbors), p82_1 (trust in police), p40 (constitution upheld), and p34 (democratic governance rating) form a theoretically coherent cluster around generalized institutional and civic trust. This 7-item set is expected to yield higher internal consistency because all items share a common attitudinal core: confidence in the integrity and effectiveness of formal and civic institutions. p34 and p40 are particularly likely to correlate with p41-p43 as they all assess system-level institutional performance. p81_1 and p82_1 add the interpersonal and law-enforcement trust dimensions explicitly mentioned in the construct description. The cluster size of 7 remains within the recommended 3-8 range.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Captures citizens' generalized trust in key civic and political institutions — including the electoral authority (INE), police, neighbors, and the constitutional and democratic order — reflecting confidence in the integrity and effectiveness of Mexico's formal rules and governance structures.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency because several items tap loosely related but distinct sub-dimensions: p60 (majority rule legitimacy) and p23 (conflict avoidance vs. policy implementation) are conceptually distant from the core authoritarian predisposition dimension and likely suppress alpha. From the unused items, p55 ('justification of harm/damages to defend a just cause') directly parallels p65 and p64 in tapping tolerance for rule-breaking when ends are perceived as just. p56_1 (whether authorities should make exceptions in law enforcement for certain protesters) taps the idea that rules can be selectively applied by authorities, aligning with p64. p49 and p50 are too vague without knowing their exact content, but their generic framing makes them risky additions. The strongest candidates are p55 (clearly on-theme: justifying illegal/harmful acts for a just cause) and p56_1 (selective application of law by authorities). Removing p60 (majority-rule legitimacy is more a democratic norms item than authoritarianism) and p23 (conflict-avoidance framing is orthogonal to the authoritarian core) should tighten the cluster. The retained core — p61 (strong leader over laws), p64 (how authorities should pursue justice), p65 (breaking law to act correctly), p17 (freedom vs. security trade-off) — plus p55 and p56_1 forms a more coherent set around the themes of rule-of-law skepticism, executive power preference, and ends-justify-means reasoning.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures citizens' preference for strong executive leadership over legal constraints, tolerance for bypassing or selectively applying rules to achieve just ends, willingness to subordinate individual freedoms to security or order, and acceptance of extralegal means when pursuing perceived justice.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α = 0.393) likely because p44_1 (perception of family tolerance) taps a different referent (family attitudes) than p47 and p48 (personal justification and authority response to exclusion). The three items also mix attitudinal levels (personal perception, normative belief, policy preference) without a unifying behavioral or attitudinal anchor. From the unused pool, several items directly measure acceptance of religious diversity at the individual behavioral level: p46_1 (willingness to allow people of another religion to live in one's home) is a direct behavioral-intention measure of personal religious tolerance and should strongly cohere with p47 and p48. p40 (belief that religion causes divisions among people) taps the perceived social consequences of religious difference, which is conceptually adjacent to tolerance/intolerance of diversity. p34 (belief that one can freely choose their religion in Mexico) reflects perceived religious freedom in the social environment, complementing the exclusion items. p50_1 (personal experience of rights violations due to religion) adds a lived-experience dimension consistent with minority religious status and tolerance. Together, p46_1, p40, p34, and p50_1 anchor the construct more firmly in acceptance/rejection of religious diversity at both personal and societal levels. p44_1 is flagged for removal because it measures perceived family attitudes rather than the respondent's own tolerance, introducing a different referent that weakens coherence. The revised cluster (p47, p48, p46_1, p40, p34, p50_1) should yield improved internal consistency by focusing on the respondent's own acceptance or rejection of religious diversity and minority rights.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which individuals personally accept religious diversity in their social environment, including willingness to cohabit with people of other religions, justification for expelling religious minorities from communities, opinions on authority responses to majority-minority religious conflicts, perceived freedom of religion in society, belief that religion causes social divisions, and personal experience of rights violations based on religion.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.481) because its three items tap conceptually adjacent but psychometrically distinct facets: perceived corruption (p28_1), perceived human rights violations (p29_1), and normative compliance (p53). The corruption and HR-violation items likely correlate moderately with each other but weakly with the normative-compliance item, which is more abstract and attitudinal. To build a coherent police institutional legitimacy scale, I recommend: (1) KEEPING p28_1 and p29_1 as they both tap perceived police misconduct/integrity; (2) REPLACING p53 (normative acceptance even when wrong — a coercive-compliance framing that is conceptually orthogonal to trust/legitimacy) with items that share the same evaluative referent (municipal police) and the same response logic; (3) ADDING p26_1 (performance rating 0-10), p27_1 (satisfaction with performance), p30_1 (crime-prevention effectiveness rating), and p52 (agreement that police are legitimate authority and should be obeyed). Together, p28_1, p29_1, p26_1, p27_1, p30_1, and p52 all evaluate the same target institution (municipal/local police) on dimensions of integrity, performance, and legitimacy — the classic three-component model of police legitimacy (Tyler & Huo). p52 is a direct legitimacy-acceptance item framed positively ('police are legitimate authority'), unlike p53 which frames compliance as deference even when wrong, making p52 far more likely to co-vary with the performance and integrity items. p62a_1 (treatment quality during contact) was considered but introduces selection bias (only those with contact answer), so it is excluded. The resulting 6-item cluster should achieve α ≥ 0.65 based on the conceptual coherence and shared evaluative target.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' evaluations of municipal police across three legitimacy-relevant dimensions: perceived institutional integrity (corruption, human rights violations), performance quality (overall rating, satisfaction, crime-prevention effectiveness), and normative legitimacy (acceptance of police as a rightful authority). All items share the same institutional referent (municipal/preventive police) and an evaluative response logic, supporting internal consistency.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with this construct is a fundamental measurement mismatch: p22 is a binary yes/no (paid vs. not paid), p23_1 is an ordinal agreement scale about financial impact, and p48_3 is an ordinal scale about caregiving burden on income — three items with different response formats, different referents (individual payment, family finances, caregiving costs), and likely very different distributions. The near-zero or negative alpha reflects that these items are essentially uncorrelated in practice, not just poorly worded. Reviewing all unused items in the domain, none of them measure financial burden of healthcare in a way that would cohere with the existing items. The unused items cover: general health status (p1), health problem occurrence (p11), care-seeking behavior (p13), travel time (p15), treatment adherence (p18, p18_1), need for tests (p20), non-medical remedies (p21), satisfaction with care (p25_1), diet and lifestyle rankings (p26_1, p27_1), smoking (p30, p31, p32), alcohol (p33), mental health symptoms (p36_1), body image (p41), leisure time (p44, p45), physical activity (p46), environmental satisfaction (p46_2_1), caregiving need (p47), disability in household (p49), preventive screenings (p50, p51), and pain interference (p7). None of these tap out-of-pocket expenditure, financial hardship from medical costs, or economic burden of healthcare. The construct suffers from a survey design gap: there are simply no additional financial-burden items available in the unused pool. Adding conceptually unrelated items to artificially inflate alpha would destroy construct validity. The honest recommendation is that this construct cannot be salvaged with the available item pool and should either be dissolved (with p22 treated as a standalone descriptive variable and p23_1 retained as a single indicator) or flagged for questionnaire redesign to include additional financial burden items (e.g., catastrophic health expenditure, foregone care due to cost, health insurance coverage adequacy).

The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.401) partly because p20 and p21 are broad racism/skin-color perception items that are conceptually more distal from indigenous-specific discrimination than the other items. However, removing them risks losing breadth. The core problem is insufficient items tapping the same specific dimension: perceived systemic inequality and discrimination against indigenous people in Mexico. From the unused pool, several items directly measure perceived inequality of rights, opportunities, and treatment for indigenous people—exactly the same dimension. Recommended additions: (1) p30 — perception of whether indigenous human rights are respected in Mexico (directly measures systemic rights denial); (2) p32 — perception of equal employment opportunities for indigenous vs. non-indigenous people (parallel to p34_1 on employment, should correlate strongly); (3) p35_1 — belief about whether indigenous people can freely express themselves (rights/freedom dimension); (4) p38_1 — belief about whether children of mixed parentage would face discrimination (discrimination perception, closely aligned). These four additions all tap perceived discrimination and systemic inequality against indigenous people in Mexico, which should substantially raise inter-item correlations. p20 (skin color influences treatment) is the weakest fit—it is generic and not indigenous-specific—and should be removed to tighten the construct. p21 (racism exists in Mexico) is borderline but retained as an anchor for the broader discrimination perception frame. The revised cluster (p21, p25_1, p30, p32, p34_1, p35_1, p38_1) consists of 7 items all asking whether indigenous people face unequal treatment, lack of rights, or discrimination in specific domains (employment, expression, human rights, discrimination perception), which should yield substantially higher α. p20 is flagged for removal as it is race-generic rather than indigenous-specific and likely the main source of misfit.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which respondents perceive that indigenous people face discrimination, unequal treatment, and denial of rights in Mexican society, including perceptions of racism, employment inequality, restrictions on free expression, human rights violations, and anticipated discrimination—capturing recognition of systemic inequality specifically directed at indigenous populations.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency (α = 0.264) because its items tap loosely related but conceptually distinct sub-dimensions: attitudes toward indigenous customs as obstacles (p22), an underspecified agreement item (p29), self-governance vs. electoral equality (p46), and language of education (p48). The low alpha suggests respondents do not answer these items in a coherent, unidimensional way. To build a more coherent scale, I focus on a tighter sub-dimension: **normative attitudes toward indigenous autonomy, rights, and self-determination** — specifically whether indigenous peoples should have distinct rights, control over their lands, language access, and self-governance, versus being treated identically to non-indigenous citizens. From the unused items, p47 (land rights/decision to sell ancestral lands), p49 (language access to public services vs. requiring Spanish), p46 (self-governance vs. equal electoral participation — keep), and p48 (language of education — keep) form a coherent cluster around the autonomy-vs-assimilation dimension. p23 (most appropriate government action for indigenous peoples) also taps this normative policy dimension. p24_1 (equal treatment of indigenous and non-indigenous people) is relevant but may tap a different dimension (anti-discrimination rather than autonomy), so it is flagged as optional. p22 is removed because 'customs as obstacle to progress' conflates cultural prejudice with policy preference and is likely the main source of misfit. p29 is removed because its content is underspecified and likely contributes noise. The proposed core cluster — p46, p47, p48, p49, p23 — all ask respondents to choose between an autonomy/distinct-rights position and an assimilation/equal-treatment position, which should produce much higher inter-item correlations. This yields 5 items, within the 3–8 target range.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' normative policy preferences regarding indigenous peoples' rights to self-determination versus assimilation into mainstream Mexican society. Items cover: indigenous self-governance versus equal electoral participation (p46), the right of indigenous communities to decide whether to sell ancestral lands to extractive industries (p47), the appropriate language for indigenous children's education (p48), whether indigenous people should require Spanish or receive translation to access public services (p49), and the most appropriate form of government action toward indigenous peoples (p23). Higher scores indicate stronger support for indigenous autonomy and distinct collective rights; lower scores indicate preference for uniform civic integration.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current three items have poor internal consistency (α=0.319) because they tap very different response formats and dimensions: p15 is a comparative difficulty judgment, while p27_1 and p28_1 are open-ended occupational recall items that are likely coded into heterogeneous nominal categories, making them nearly impossible to correlate meaningfully with each other or with p15. The construct's stated goal—measuring mental representations, stereotypes, and social image of indigenous people—is better served by attitudinal and perceptual items with consistent Likert-style or binary response scales. From the unused pool, the following items share a coherent underlying dimension of 'perceived social standing, image, and stereotyped attributes of indigenous people' and use comparable response formats: p26_1 (family image of indigenous people—valence rating), p32 (equal employment opportunities—perception of disadvantage), p31 (school access belief—perception of capability/opportunity), p35_1 (freedom of expression for indigenous people—perceived autonomy), p44_1 (poverty attributed to lack of work—occupational/effort stereotype), and p38_1 (children of mixed parentage facing discrimination—social image consequence). These six items all ask respondents to evaluate the social position, capabilities, or image of indigenous people, and use ordinal or binary agree/disagree or yes/no formats that can be scored consistently. p27_1 and p28_1 should be removed because open-ended occupational recall items coded into nominal categories cannot produce meaningful inter-item correlations. p15 may be retained as it fits the perceived-disadvantage dimension, but its contribution is marginal; it is kept to preserve continuity. The new cluster of 6–7 items should produce substantially higher internal consistency by focusing on evaluative perceptions of indigenous people's social standing and image rather than mixing recall tasks with attitude items.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' evaluative perceptions of indigenous people's social position, opportunities, and image, including beliefs about equal access to education and employment, freedom of expression, family-level image valence, attribution of poverty to personal effort (occupational stereotype), and anticipated discrimination against mixed-heritage children. Items use consistent ordinal or binary response formats to enable reliable aggregation.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current three items are poorly matched: p33_7 (computer use for browsing) and p39_7 (mobile data access) tap into access/device behavior, while p45_2 (reporting a crime online) is a rare, highly specific civic act that shares little variance with the other two — this mismatch drives the low α. The construct's stated goal is 'functional digital competence beyond mere access,' which is best captured by items measuring self-assessed proficiency, breadth of purposeful internet activities, and active digital engagement. strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the respondent's subjective sense of how deeply embedded and inevitable corruption is in Mexico, capturing temporal trajectory assessments (past and future change), perceived difficulty of eradicating corruption, perceived normalization of bribery motivations, and overall perception of law-respect in the country. strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from severe conceptual heterogeneity: p15 (pride in being Mexican) and p11 (interest in other cultures) tap orthogonal dimensions to p19_1 (world citizen identity) and p20 (global vs. national identity framing), explaining the near-zero alpha. strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the extent to which respondents have personally engaged with the world beyond Mexico through direct behavioral indicators: international travel frequency, residence abroad, having close ties to people living abroad, willingness to emigrate, and active consumption of international news and foreign media. strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency (α=0.354) because its items tap very different facets: an affective respect rating (p24_1), a fairness evaluation (p25a_1), an open-ended opinion on preventing abuse (p59), and a procedural expectation (p40_2_1). p59 is an open-ended/qualitative item that is almost certainly unscalable alongside Likert/numeric items and should be removed immediately. p40_2_1 measures a generic service expectation rather than police-specific trust or legitimacy, making it a poor fit. strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Respondent's affective trust in and respect for police (two 0-10 numeric ratings) combined with an evaluative judgment of whether police resolve problems fairly. strategy2_reexplore.reasoning:

To improve coherence, I recommend the following changes:

4. ADD p30: What the respondent would do if a judge's sentence negatively affects their interests measures willingness to use formal legal recourse (appeal, amparo, etc.), which is conceptually central to the construct and should correlate with p35 (utility of amparo) and p39 (worthwhile to go to court).

The resulting 7-item cluster (p12, p28, p30, p35, p36, p37, p39) covers: rights knowledge, access under financial constraint, response to adverse rulings, utility of amparo, belief in suing the government, perceived chances of winning, and general court worthiness — all facets of a single underlying dimension of legal self-efficacy and access to justice.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Respondent's perceived capacity to navigate the legal system, encompassing self-assessed knowledge of personal rights, beliefs about the utility and feasibility of formal legal recourse (including amparo and suing the government), perceived likelihood of success in legal disputes, access to legal representation under financial constraints, and behavioral intentions when facing adverse judicial outcomes.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with the current cluster is that it mixes a conditional willingness item (p15_1: work-specific), a general desire item (p17), and a probability/behavioral intention item (p18) — these tap slightly different facets and the binary/scale format mismatch reduces coherence. The open-ended items (p19_1, p20_1a_1) are not scalable. p6_1 (justification for undocumented migration) is the closest attitudinal item that could correlate with personal intention, as respondents who justify undocumented migration are more likely to consider emigrating themselves. Removing p15_1 and adding p6_1 may modestly improve internal consistency by making the remaining items more conceptually uniform around personal emigration disposition, though a high alpha is unlikely given the small item pool and format heterogeneity. Confidence is low because without item-level correlation data, this is a theoretically motivated recommendation rather than an empirically verified one.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the respondent's personal disposition toward emigrating from Mexico, capturing normative justification for leaving, desire to live abroad, and anticipated probability of emigrating within five years.

strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' attitudinal beliefs about the conditions and outcomes experienced by Mexican emigrants, including perceived discrimination in the United States, quality of jobs held by Mexicans abroad, general assessments of what happens to Mexicans who move abroad, the rights they should hold, and whether Mexicans who study abroad should return to Mexico. This 6-item cluster should yield substantially improved internal consistency. Items like p68_1 (perceived control over personal finances) were considered but reflect individual agency rather than dyadic power dynamics, so were excluded. p34 and p35 relate to physical violence rather than power dynamics and were excluded.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the structural balance of power within romantic partnerships, capturing financial control and transparency (who manages money, mutual knowledge of incomes), household decision-making authority, and behavioral autonomy (frequency of seeking partner's permission for daily activities and family visits).

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from severe conceptual heterogeneity: p32 measures prevalence beliefs, p33 measures personal experience, p65 measures legal awareness, and only p31 touches on attitudinal tolerance — none of these tap the same latent dimension, explaining the extremely low α. From the unused items, three stand out as measuring attitudinal tolerance/justification of gender-based violence directly: p35 ('In what circumstances is hitting a woman justified?') is the clearest direct measure of tolerance of violence against women; p34 ('What would you do if your partner physically assaulted you?') captures behavioral intention that reflects implicit tolerance or rejection of intimate partner violence; p38 and p39 (childhood physical punishment by father/mother) tap intergenerational normalization of physical violence in family contexts, which is conceptually adjacent. p10_1 (equal treatment of boys and girls) measures gender egalitarianism, a theoretically related attitudinal dimension that often co-varies with tolerance of GBV. The recommended core is p35 (direct justification), p34 (response to assault — tolerance vs. rejection), p31 (emotional reaction to verbal violence, retained as it is attitudinal), and p38/p39 (normalization of family violence through childhood experience). p32, p33, and p65 should be removed: p32 is a factual prevalence belief, p33 is a personal experience indicator (better suited to a victimization construct), and p65 is a knowledge/awareness item with no attitudinal loading. This revised set of 5 items (p31, p34, p35, p38, p39) shares a coherent underlying dimension — the degree to which physical violence in intimate/family contexts is normalized, justified, or tolerated — and should yield substantially improved internal consistency. Confidence is medium because item response formats vary and empirical confirmation via piloting is needed.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which respondents justify, tolerate, or normalize physical and verbal violence in intimate partner and family contexts, including direct justification of hitting women, intended responses to partner assault, emotional reactions to witnessed verbal aggression, and exposure to physical punishment in the family of origin as an indicator of intergenerational normalization.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current cluster suffers from poor internal consistency because it mixes conceptually distinct item types: a behavioral/knowledge item (p46_1, condom use/knowledge), an opinion item about sex work involving minors (p47, which taps into child protection norms more than reproductive attitudes), and attitudinal items about premarital sex, adolescent pregnancy options, and decision-making authority. These items do not load onto a single coherent attitudinal dimension. To build a more coherent cluster, I recommend: (1) Removing p46_1 (behavioral/knowledge measure, not an attitude) and p47 (taps child protection/exploitation norms, a distinct dimension). (2) Retaining p45 (premarital sex attitudes), p50 (response to unplanned adolescent pregnancy), and p51 (decision-making authority over pregnancy continuation) as the attitudinal core. (3) Adding p48 (perception of adolescent pregnancy as a national problem — normative concern about adolescent sexuality), p52 (knowledge of abortion legality — reflects engagement with reproductive rights context and correlates with attitudes), p53 (opinion on quality of abortion medical care — directly taps reproductive rights attitudes), and p55_1 (willingness to marry a divorced person — reflects normative attitudes about sexuality, family formation, and social acceptance of non-traditional sexual/reproductive outcomes). Together, p45, p48, p50, p51, p52, p53, and p55_1 form a more coherent attitudinal cluster around reproductive norms, rights, and decision-making. p52 and p53 are particularly valuable as they directly address abortion attitudes — a core reproductive rights dimension. p55_1 adds a related social norm about sexual/marital history. This revised set should yield substantially improved internal consistency by anchoring all items to normative beliefs about reproductive and sexual decision-making rather than mixing knowledge, behavior, and attitude item types.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures normative attitudes toward sexual behavior, reproductive decision-making, and reproductive rights, including views on premarital sex, unplanned adolescent pregnancy, abortion legality and access, and social acceptance of non-traditional sexual and family outcomes in Mexico.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current cluster has extremely poor internal consistency (α = 0.239) because its three items measure conceptually distinct things: willingness to vote for a woman (political behavior), perceived difficulty of life by gender (comparative hardship), and attitudes toward homosexuality (sexual orientation norms). These tap different dimensions and will not cohere. The recommended solution is to remove all three current items and build a new cluster around attitudinal items that more directly and consistently measure support for gender equality and egalitarian norms. From the unused pool, the strongest candidates are: p10_1 (boys and girls should be treated equally — core gender egalitarianism), p35 (circumstances justifying hitting a woman — inverse measure of gender-based violence acceptance, strongly linked to gender equality attitudes), p66 (exposure to gender equality campaigns — behavioral/awareness indicator of gender equality engagement), and p44 (preference for sex of first child — measures son-preference bias, a well-validated proxy for gender egalitarianism in Latin American survey research). Together these four items share a common latent dimension: endorsement of gender equality norms across domestic, social, and public spheres. p9 is removed because 'life is harder for whom' conflates gender awareness with zero-sum framing and loads poorly with behavioral items. p41_1 (vote for a woman) is removed because willingness-to-vote items in this region tend to have ceiling effects and low variance, suppressing covariance. p42_1 (homosexuality is normal) is removed because it measures sexual orientation tolerance, a related but empirically distinct dimension (LGB rights vs. gender equality) that consistently forms a separate factor in Latin American survey data. p54_1 (children need both parents) was considered but reflects family structure conservatism more than gender equality per se. p60_3 (men need a friend to talk to) was considered but measures masculine emotional norms rather than gender equality support. The four recommended items (p10_1, p35, p44, p66) are all closed-ended, use comparable Likert or categorical response scales, and are theoretically grounded in the same latent construct of gender egalitarianism. Confidence is medium rather than high because α improvement cannot be guaranteed without empirical testing — p35 and p44 may require reverse-coding and the sample's variance on p10_1 may be limited if responses cluster at the 'agree' end.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures endorsement of gender egalitarian norms across domestic and social domains, including equal treatment of boys and girls, rejection of gender-based violence justifications, absence of son-preference bias, and engagement with gender equality campaigns.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The construct aims to measure early cultural capital transmission through parental behavior and childhood environment. The current α=0.480 is likely depressed because p12_1 (respondent's own independent reading behavior) taps a different facet (child agency) rather than parental/environmental provision of cultural resources. Removing p12_1 and adding p13 (presence of books in the home during childhood) and p9_1 (parents taking child to museums) strengthens the construct by focusing it tightly on the family environment and parental cultural investment during childhood. p13 is a classic Bourdieusian indicator of objectified cultural capital in the home, highly correlated with p11 (book gifting) and p8 (parental cultural discussion). p9_1 (museum visits with parents) is a direct behavioral indicator of parental cultural socialization. Together, p8 + p11 + p13 + p9_1 form a coherent 4-item cluster all measuring the same latent dimension: the degree to which parents actively provided a culturally rich environment during childhood. p41_g1 (mother's education) was considered as a proxy for cultural capital transmission but is a background variable rather than a direct behavioral measure, so it was excluded to maintain construct purity. p12_1 is flagged for removal because independent reading by the child reflects the child's own behavior/outcome rather than parental socialization inputs, introducing construct-irrelevant variance.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures the degree to which respondents were exposed to culturally enriching environments and parental cultural investment during childhood, including parental discussion of culture, book gifting, presence of books in the home, and family visits to cultural institutions such as museums.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct has extremely poor internal consistency (α = 0.139) because its three items tap fundamentally different response formats and cognitive tasks: an open-ended sport name (p19_2a_1), an opinion about youth motivation (p20), and a categorical level-of-competition question (p21_1). These share almost no common variance. The fix requires building a coherent sports engagement cluster around behaviorally anchored, similarly-scaled items. From the unused pool, the strongest candidates are: p19 (binary: currently practices sport — the logical behavioral anchor), p21 (frequency of watching sports on TV in past two weeks — behavioral media consumption, continuous), and p22_1 (opinion on the purpose/function of sport — attitudinal, parallels p20 on social function of sport). Together with p20 (opinion on what inspires youth to practice sport), p19 and p21 form a tighter behavioral core (active participation + passive media consumption), while p22_1 adds a coherent attitudinal dimension about sport's social function that pairs naturally with p20. Removing p19_2a_1 (open-ended sport name — essentially a follow-up probe with no variance structure) and p21_1 (competition level — a conditional follow-up with very restricted, nominal variance) eliminates the main sources of incoherence. The resulting 4-item cluster (p19, p21, p20, p22_1) covers: sport practice behavior, sport media behavior, beliefs about youth motivation in sport, and beliefs about sport's social function — a conceptually unified sports engagement and attitudes dimension with compatible response formats (binary/frequency/opinion) that should yield substantially higher internal consistency.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' active sports participation, consumption of sports media (television), and their beliefs about the social function of sport and what motivates youth to engage in sports activities.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.489) likely because p3 (intergenerational comparison with parents) and p2 (personal economic satisfaction) load on a somewhat different dimension than p1 (country-level satisfaction), and the generational comparison items introduce additional variance. To improve coherence, I recommend adding p4 (perceived future economic standard for children), which directly parallels p3 and extends the intergenerational mobility theme, creating a tighter 'perceived economic mobility and trajectory' sub-cluster alongside the satisfaction items. Additionally, p6_1 (perceived impact of insecurity on economic advancement) taps into macro-economic evaluation consistent with p1, and p9_1 (government priority to prevent price increases) reflects personal economic concern consistent with p2. Together, these additions reinforce both the personal and societal economic evaluation dimensions. p5 (attribution of responsibility for the economy) is tempting but is open-ended and categorical, reducing scale coherence. p4 is the strongest addition as it directly mirrors p3's comparative format and shares the same response scale, which should substantially boost inter-item correlation with p3. p6_1 adds a macro-economic perception item that correlates conceptually with p1. The three current items are retained since removing any single one risks losing construct breadth; however, if α remains low after adding p4 and p6_1, p3 alone (without p4) would be the candidate for removal as the intergenerational comparison may be too cognitively distinct from satisfaction ratings. Cluster size moves from 3 to 5 items, within the recommended 3–8 range.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures how individuals evaluate their own and the country's economic situation, including perceived threats to economic progress (e.g., insecurity), and intergenerational comparisons of living standards across past and future generations. Captures personal and societal economic satisfaction alongside perceived upward or downward mobility.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency (α=0.376) because it mixes conceptually distinct elements: awareness of rights (p17_1), normative opinions on what rights children should have (p19, p21_2), how children should be categorized (p20), perceived respect for rights in Mexico (p22), and a youth-specific legal right (p73_3). The item p73_3 (youth right to trial) is particularly misfit as it shifts to a different age group and a narrow legal context. p19 and p20 tap normative/philosophical views rather than perceptions of recognition and respect. To build a coherent scale measuring 'perceived recognition and respect of children's and adolescents' rights in Mexico,' I recommend: (1) REMOVING p19 (normative opinion on what rights children should have — measures ideal preferences, not perceptions of reality), p20 (categorization of children — conceptually distant), and p73_3 (youth trial right — different population, narrow legal item). (2) ADDING p47 (opinion on what rights adolescents should have — parallel to p19 but for adolescents; however, same concern applies), p48_1 (agreement that adolescent rights diminish parental authority — directly taps attitudes toward rights recognition in a social context), p38 (how much adolescents' opinions should be considered in family decisions — taps recognition of adolescent voice/rights in practice), p39 (how much adolescents' opinions should be considered in school decisions — parallel recognition item), and p44 (agreement with lowering age of criminal responsibility — inversely taps respect for adolescent rights). Together with retained items p17_1 (children don't know their rights) and p22 (how much children's rights are respected in Mexico), these items form a more coherent cluster around attitudes toward the recognition, respect, and scope of children's and adolescents' rights in Mexican society. Items p38, p39, p44, and p48_1 all tap the social and institutional recognition of youth rights and are likely to co-vary with p22 and p17_1. Cluster size is 7 items, within the recommended range.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures respondents' attitudes toward the recognition, respect, and scope of children's and adolescents' rights in Mexico, including perceptions of how well rights are known and upheld, the extent to which youth voices should be considered in family and school decisions, and views on the limits and social implications of those rights.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency largely because p16_1 is an open-ended 'problems with participation' item (nominal/categorical) that does not share the same Likert-scale response format as p12 and p13, creating format-driven variance heterogeneity. The most direct fix is to remove p16_1 and add the parallel adolescent-focused participation items (p38, p39, p41, p47) which measure the same normative beliefs about youth agency and voice — just for an older age group — and share the same attitudinal response format. p38 and p39 are near-perfect parallels of p12 and p13 for adolescents, which should dramatically improve inter-item covariance. p41 (public marches/mobilizations) and p47 (what rights/freedoms adolescents should have) extend the construct into civic and rights-based participation, consistent with the construct description's reference to 'civic inclusion.' p50 (how adolescents should be categorized in society) also taps normative framing of youth agency and could be included, but risks adding noise if its response options are categorical. The core recommended set is p12, p13, p38, p39, p41, and p47, dropping p16_1. This gives a 6-item scale with consistent attitudinal format spanning children and adolescents across family, school, and civic domains — directly matching the construct description.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures normative attitudes toward the extent to which children and adolescents should have a say in decisions affecting them — in family, school, and public life — capturing beliefs about youth agency, civic inclusion, and rights across both childhood and adolescence.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from poor internal consistency (α=0.234) because its three items tap very different response formats and cognitive tasks: p25 asks about location of violence (categorical/open), p26_1 asks about justification for hitting (open-ended normative), and p27 asks about family problems (open-ended list). These are too heterogeneous to cohere. The core underlying dimension is 'perceived vulnerability of children/adolescents to violence' — a belief-based attitudinal dimension. From the unused items, p24 ('children more exposed to violence than other groups') and p52 ('adolescents more exposed to violence than other groups') are direct Likert-style belief items about violence exposure that parallel each other structurally. p53 ('where adolescents experience most violence') parallels p25 structurally and thematically. p26_1 (justification for hitting a child) is the most normative/attitudinal item and is worth retaining as it reflects acceptance of violence toward children. p27 (family problems for children) is too broad and diffuse — it is the primary misfit item and should be removed. p25 (location of violence for children) is retained as it pairs well with p53 (location for adolescents), creating parallel measurement across age groups. The revised cluster — p24, p25, p26_1, p52, p53 — forms a coherent set of beliefs about violence exposure and location across children and adolescents, with p26_1 anchoring the normative acceptance dimension. This 5-item cluster has strong conceptual alignment and parallel structure likely to improve internal consistency.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures beliefs about the relative vulnerability of children and adolescents to violence compared to other population groups, the contexts and locations where such violence occurs, and normative acceptance of physical punishment, reflecting attitudes toward child and adolescent protection and safety.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem is that p81_2 (family formation opportunities) is conceptually misaligned with educational and labor intergenerational mobility — it taps a different life domain and likely drives the low alpha. Removing it is the primary fix. The unused pool contains several highly parallel items: p57_1 asks the same intergenerational education question as p28_1 but for adolescents (not children), p81_1 asks the same intergenerational education question for young people, and p82 asks directly about job prospects for young people vs. 30 years ago — all measuring the same 'are today's youth better or worse off than previous generations?' dimension. These three additions, combined with the two retained items (p28_1 and p83_1), form a tight, conceptually coherent cluster of intergenerational comparative beliefs across education and employment for children, adolescents, and youth. p81_2 is flagged for removal as family formation is a distinct life domain that fragments the construct. The resulting 5-item cluster (p28_1, p57_1, p81_1, p82, p83_1) should achieve substantially higher internal consistency given their near-identical question stems and shared intergenerational comparison frame.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures perceptions of whether today's children, adolescents, and young people have better or worse access to education and quality employment compared to previous generations, capturing intergenerational mobility beliefs across the full youth lifecycle (children, adolescents, and young adults).

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with the current cluster is that p13–p16 are conceptually related (ideal family type, barriers, solutions, definitions) but p67 and p68 ask about intergenerational change and future change — these tap a different dimension (perceived family change/evolution) rather than normative ideology per se, which dilutes coherence. More critically, open-ended items about barriers (p14), solutions (p15), and future changes (p68) are likely coded into heterogeneous categories that produce low inter-item correlations. The recommended strategy is to anchor the construct more tightly around normative beliefs about family structure and roles, replacing the diffuse 'barriers/solutions/future' items with items that more directly probe normative standards: ideal number of children (p44), what people seek in forming a family (p19), characteristics of good parents (p60_1, p61_1), age children should leave home (p30), and the impact of mothers working (p43). These all share the underlying dimension of 'what a family should look like and how it should function' — i.e., traditional vs. pluralist family ideology. p13 and p16 are retained as anchors for family structure normativity. p14, p15, p67, and p68 are removed because they ask about external barriers, policy solutions, and temporal change rather than the respondent's own normative standards, making them conceptually and statistically misfit for this dimension. p19 and p44 tap into the normative purpose and composition of family. p43 taps gender-role normativity within the family. p30 taps normative expectations about family lifecycle boundaries. p60_1 and p61_1 tap idealized parental role standards. Together these items form a tighter nomological net around 'what an ideal family looks like and how its members should behave,' which should improve internal consistency.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Captures respondents' normative beliefs about ideal family structure, composition, and member roles — including ideal family type, ideal number of children, the purpose of forming a family, gender roles (mothers working), parental role ideals, and expectations about children's residential independence — reflecting an underlying traditionalism versus pluralism dimension in family ideology.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The core problem with the current scale is that p62 (personal preparations for old age) and p63 (who would care for respondent) are self-referential behavioral/planning items that tap a different dimension (personal contingency planning) rather than normative beliefs about intergenerational obligations. They likely suppress internal consistency. From the unused items, p30 ('until what age should children remain in parents' home') is a direct normative belief about intergenerational co-residence and is conceptually the strongest addition — it directly parallels p28 and p29 in asking about normative obligations across generations. p64 (willingness to care for a disabled family member) is the best-fitting current item alongside p28 and p29 as it captures the reciprocal duty dimension normatively. Removing p62 and p63 eliminates the behavioral/planning mismatch. The resulting cluster of p28, p29, p30, and p64 forms a tighter normative-belief scale about intergenerational duties: parental duties to children (p28), children's duties to parents (p29), co-residence norms (p30), and personal caregiving obligation (p64). No other unused items (p31 is open-ended 'why', p49 measures relationship quality not obligation norms, p60_1/p61_1 measure parenting characteristics not intergenerational duty) fit the normative obligation dimension closely enough to add without risk. Keeping the cluster at 4 items focused on normative beliefs should improve coherence and raise α above 0.5.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Measures normative beliefs about reciprocal duties between parents and children across generations, including parental obligations toward children, children's obligations toward parents, appropriate age for children to leave the parental home, and willingness to provide care for a disabled family member — capturing attitudinal endorsement of familial solidarity norms rather than personal behavioral intentions.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current cluster suffers from poor internal consistency because its three items tap fundamentally different things: p54 asks about a personally learned value (autobiographical recall), p55 asks about normative beliefs on parental transmission (opinion), and p57 asks about religion's influence on family life (institutional/sociological assessment). p57 is the clearest misfit — it shifts from value content to an institutional actor and is conceptually distant from the other two. To build a coherent cluster around 'family as a moral socialization institution,' the best strategy is to anchor items on (a) normative beliefs about what good parents do/transmit, and (b) role-model characteristics parents should embody. p60_1 (characteristics of a good father) and p61_1 (characteristics of a good mother) are directly about parental role ideals and will co-vary with p55 (values parents should transmit) because respondents draw on the same normative schema when answering all three. p56 (institution with greatest influence on family life) is a plausible addition as it contextualizes the family's socialization role relative to other institutions, but it risks adding noise. The tightest, most coherent core is p55 + p60_1 + p61_1, with p54 retained as a behaviorally grounded anchor of the same value-transmission schema. p57 should be removed. This four-item set (p54, p55, p60_1, p61_1) all require respondents to activate the same normative mental model of what families should teach and what good parents look like, maximizing shared variance and expected α.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Captures respondents' normative beliefs about the family as a moral socialization institution: the values personally learned and practiced from one's family of origin (p54), the fundamental values parents should transmit to children (p55), and the ideal characteristics of a good father (p60_1) and a good mother (p61_1). Together these items reflect a shared cognitive schema about parental role models and value transmission.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct suffers from low internal consistency (α=0.303) because its three items tap conceptually adjacent but psychometrically distinct facets: a trend perception item (p49), a life-skills relevance item (p51), and a school-to-work relevance item for current students (p20). The core problem is that p20 and p51 are near-parallel in content but p49 (trend over time) loads on a different cognitive task (retrospective comparison), and p20 applies only to current students while p33 asks the same question for those who have left school, fragmenting the sample. To build a coherent 'perceived education quality' scale, the strategy is: (1) REMOVE p20 (narrow applicability, near-duplicate of p33 for a different sub-population, likely causing variance restriction); (2) KEEP p49 (trend perception) and p51 (life relevance); (3) ADD p52 (0-10 numeric quality rating — directly operationalises the quality evaluation dimension and is the most face-valid anchor for the construct); (4) ADD p53_1 (primary problem identified with education — inverse indicator of perceived quality; respondents who rate quality poorly tend to name more/severe problems, providing discriminant variance that tightens the scale); (5) ADD p33 (school-to-work relevance for those who have left school — conceptually identical to p20 but for the larger out-of-school sub-sample; combining p33 and p51 gives broader coverage of the 'relevance' facet across both populations). Together p49 + p51 + p52 + p53_1 + p33 form a coherent evaluative cluster around 'how good is Mexican education and is it relevant?' — all require the respondent to make a quality judgment about the educational system, which should substantially raise inter-item correlations. p54_1 (government actions needed) was considered but reflects prescriptive policy preference rather than quality evaluation and was excluded to keep the construct tight. p20 is flagged for removal because it duplicates p33 for a restricted sub-group, introducing missing data and reducing covariance.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Respondent's overall evaluative judgment of the quality of education in Mexico, encompassing perceived change over the past decade, a numeric quality rating, identification of the main problems in Mexican education, and beliefs about whether schooling is relevant to real life and to work.

strategy2_reexplore.reasoning: The current construct has very poor internal consistency (α = 0.310) because its three items tap conceptually distinct sub-dimensions: universal access as a belief (p55_1, p55_2) and gender discrimination (p39_1). These do not co-vary reliably. From the unused items, the most coherent replacement cluster centers on perceptions of systemic barriers and equity problems in education — items that all ask the respondent to evaluate or judge the educational system's fairness and quality from an external, evaluative stance. Specifically: p52 (overall quality rating of education in Mexico), p53_1 (main problems in Mexican education), p54_1 (what government should do to improve education), and p40_1 (physical access barriers for students with disabilities) all share the same evaluative-perceptual frame about systemic educational equity and quality. p38 (why youth neither study nor work) also fits this systemic-barriers framing. Together these items ask respondents to assess structural deficiencies in the educational system, which is the true underlying dimension of 'educational inclusion and equity as perceived systemic problems.' p55_1 and p55_2 are retained as anchors since they directly address universal access beliefs, but p39_1 (gender discrimination) is removed as it loads on a different attitudinal dimension (gender bias) that diverges from the access/quality cluster. The replacement set — p55_1, p55_2, p52, p53_1, p54_1, p40_1 — should yield higher inter-item covariance because all items ask respondents to evaluate whether the educational system is fair, adequate, and accessible for all, producing a coherent 'perceived educational equity and quality' construct. p38 is added as it directly reflects perceived structural barriers preventing educational participation.
strategy2_reexplore.suggested_description: Respondent's evaluative perceptions of the fairness, accessibility, and quality of the educational system in Mexico, including beliefs about universal access and capacity, overall quality ratings, identification of systemic problems, government responsibilities for improvement, barriers faced by students with disabilities, and structural causes of educational exclusion among youth.
